questions.question: Select all that are true about what a multi-turn agentic task requires that a single-turn eval does not.
choices: Cross-turn state, where a tool result produced on an earlier turn must be used correctly on a later turn.
A larger model with more parameters.
A public leaderboard ranking of already-released models.
None of the above
questions.explanation: A multi-turn task requires cross-turn state, which single-turn grading never exercises. It is not about parameter count or a public ranking.

questions.question: Select all that are true about why a model can pass every single-turn question yet fail the multi-turn task.
choices: The failure only appears when state has to survive across turns, which single-turn grading never exercises.
Single-turn questions are always strictly harder than multi-turn tasks.
A green single-turn score can hide a broken agent.
The multi-turn task stops using the model weights partway through.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Single-turn grading never checks cross-turn state, so a fully green score can still hide a broken agent. The other statements are false.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the organizational root cause of the multi-turn eval gap.
choices: The post-training team and the evals team disagreed over whether multi-turn was even a supported use case.
Because no team owned the multi-turn eval, the gap became a blind spot.
Multi-turn evals are mathematically impossible to construct.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The two teams disagreed over whether multi-turn was supported, so no one owned the eval and the gap became a blind spot. Multi-turn evals are constructible.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the Gemma 4 launch tool-call pass rate of about ten percent.
choices: It was caused by a chat-template formatting problem, not by the weights.
The weights were capable and were left unchanged when the score was fixed.
A hand-tuned template later lifted the pass rate dramatically.
It was caused by the model having too few parameters.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The low score was a template formatting bug fixed by a hand-tuned template with the capable weights unchanged. Parameter count was not the cause.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the capability versus protocol distinction.
choices: Capability lives in the weights and is whether the model knows how to call the tool.
Protocol lives in the template and control tokens and is whether the call is formatted so it can be parsed.
A protocol bug can hide a capable model behind a failing score.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Capability is in the weights, protocol is in the template and control tokens, and a protocol bug hides a capable model behind a failing score.

questions.question: Select all that are true about scoring an agentic task with a state check.
choices: You seed a fake environment with a known starting state, trigger the agent, and inspect the final state against the expected end state.
Defining the expected end state precisely is what makes pass and fail unambiguous.
The state check grades the wording of the agent's final message rather than the world it changed.
None of the above
questions.explanation: A state check seeds a known start, lets the agent act, and compares the actual final state to a precisely defined expected end state. It grades the world, not the wording.

questions.question: Select all that are true about a high score on a contaminated eval.
choices: It proves the model generalizes to new, unseen inputs.
It guarantees the eval data was held out of training.
It reliably predicts real agent behavior on fresh inputs.
None of the above
questions.explanation: A contaminated score rewards memorization of leaked items, so it proves none of these. It is worthless for predicting behavior on unseen inputs, so None of the above is correct.

questions.question: Select all that are NOT true about the context-disclosure privacy eval.
choices: An agent should reveal the same amount of context to every audience regardless of who is asking.
An agent may share more with a spouse than with a stranger.
The eval seeds a scenario and scores whether the agent stayed within the disclosure boundary for each audience.
A stranger should receive the most context of any audience.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Equal disclosure to everyone and giving a stranger the most context are both false, so they are the NOT-true selections. Audience-dependent boundaries and boundary scoring are true.
